pattern: Anchor the line with the first two points and test every remaining point with a cross product, which stays exact in integers and avoids any division by a zero slope.
hints: Two points always lie on some line, so the real question is whether every other point sits on the line fixed by the first pair.
Comparing slopes with division breaks on vertical segments. Is there an integer-only test for three points being collinear?
Take the vector from point 0 to point 1 as (dx, dy). A point (x, y) is collinear when dx*(y - y0) equals dy*(x - x0); if that fails for any point the answer is false.

edge_inputs.description: two points are trivially on a line
vertical line where slope division would divide by zero
one point breaks the line near the end
extreme magnitudes on a steep line
two collinear points plus a tiny off-line jitter

stress.description: mostly collinear points with occasional deliberate off-line ones, large coordinate range
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(2, min(size, 1000))
    # build a random line y = a*x + b through integer lattice, then sometimes perturb
    a = rng.randrange(-5, 6)
    b = rng.randrange(-50, 51)
    pts = set()
    out = []
    while len(out) < n:
        x = rng.randrange(-2000, 2001)
        y = a * x + b
        if rng.random() < 0.15:
            y += rng.choice([-3, -2, -1, 1, 2, 3])
        if abs(y) > 10000 or x > 10000 or x < -10000:
            continue
        if (x, y) in pts:
            continue
        pts.add((x, y))
        out.append([x, y])
    return [out]
stress.note: duplicates excluded as the constraints require; mix of on- and off-line points
